source.title: TokenCache stride prefetch and long-context ops review - EmberTech
next_steps: Ethan to share stride-prefetch benchmark script and staging creds by 2026-08-16
EmberTech to run 24h TokenCache stress test with stride=1,2,4 and report eviction rates (due 2026-08-21)
Schedule on-prem network egress review with EmberTech infra (tentative week of 2026-08-24)
Marco to prepare a short note on KV cold-start heuristics and recommended defaults
action_items: Ethan: send benchmark scripts + example telemetry dashboard link by 2026-08-16
Aisha: provide representative workload traces (500 sessions) from staging by 2026-08-18
Marco: draft prefetch tuning playbook and share with EmberTech by 2026-08-20
David: enable extended logs for KV page events for 72h and share results
source.summary: Deep-dive on TokenCache behavior under EmberTech's long-context assistant workload. Focus on stride prefetching, eviction spikes during burst traffic, prefix reuse patterns, and operational telemetry to isolate paging events. Agreed to run targeted stress tests and exchange traces; Redwood to provide tuning playbook and scripts.
topics: On-prem / VPC considerations for KV hotset
source.transcript:
[00:00] Ethan (Redwood): okay, good afternoon everyone, thanks for taking the time. we got—uh—roughly an hour, hour and a bit, to go through the TokenCache behavior you saw in staging. Lina and Marco are on for the deep bits. Aisha, David, Rita, hi. could you confirm you can see the deck?
[00:18] Aisha (EmberTech): yep, we can see it, looks good. thanks for scheduling. we've been seeing these eviction spikes during our long-context flows, particularly when agents spawn multiple side-chats.
[00:32] Lina (Redwood SE): cool, can you remind us which model variant and token lengths you run? last note we had was a 32k context optimized LLM, quantized 4-bit, is that still accurate?
[00:44] David (EmberTech): yep, 32k variant, q4_hybrid-ish. average request is around 6k tokens, but there are frequent sessions hitting 18k+ due to chain-of-thought traces and multi-turn summarizations.
[00:59] Marco (Redwood): alright, that explains the hotset dynamics. quick primer — our TokenCache stores KV by prefix shards and supports stride prefetching where the runtime will prefetch every Nth block of the sequence to reduce cold misses. stride=1 is eager prefetch, stride=4 is sparser.
[01:18] Rita (EmberTech): we tried increasing stride to 8 to save memory and saw lower memory pressure but latency tail increased, and for some sessions the model started to thrash and evict repeatedly.
[01:31] Lina (Redwood SE): that's the classic tradeoff — stride controls how much of the forward context we keep resident. with large long-context sessions, high stride reduces cache residency and increases page-in events when the KV is needed mid-generation. evictions may cascade if your eviction threshold is absolute rather than LRU-weighted by recent access.
[01:51] Aisha: can you explain 'cascade' in practical terms? we saw sometimes CPU spikes then whole node GC? it looked like memory was oscillating and the app wrote to disk.
[02:03] Marco: yeah, by cascade I mean: one prefix gets evicted, then a following token requires that prefix, requests re-trigger a page-in, page-in consumes IO and pushes other prefetched pages out because memory was already constrained, then more page-ins — it's a feedback loop. that can show up as IO throughput spikes and CPU stall if your node is trying to compress or deserialize many small segments.
[02:28] Speaker 1: (unlabeled) and sometimes the transcription here calls it K-Vee cache, which is fine, we saw that in the logs.
[02:36] Ethan: haha, yeah. okay, so let's talk measurement. what telemetry do you currently have? eviction counters, page-in timestamps, prefix hit ratio?
[02:45] David: we have high-level metrics — overall evictions per minute, memory usage per node, and tail latency percentiles. not much on per-prefix hit ratio; we didn't want to add too much cardinality to metrics.
[02:57] Lina: per-prefix can be heavy, but we recommend sampling and histograms. even a sampled prefix-hit distribution will show whether a small hotset accounts for most hits. often with multi-agent flows a small number of prefixes are reused heavily — prefix reuse — and that's where targeted pinning helps.
[03:16] Aisha: pinning? like forcing some prefixes to never evict? we worried about memory permanence if we pin too many.
[03:25] Marco: yes, pinning is selective: pin the top N prefixes by access frequency or session-critical prefixes like initial system prompts. combined with a TTL-based fallback you can avoid permanent leak. we typically implement soft-pin: prefer to keep but allow eviction if memory pressure is extreme.
[03:44] Rita: what about prefetch policy changes? we tried 'lookahead' prefetch in our layer and it seemed to reduce misses but increased average latency by 8-10ms. is that expected?
[03:54] Lina: yes, prefetch trades memory and CPU for latency smoothing. lookahead often fetches blocks likely to be used in the next steps; it adds some CPU/IO overhead up-front but reduces mid-generation page-ins that cause larger stalls. the 8-10ms you saw is plausible; the key is tail vs p50. if it reduces 99.9th from 1.2s to 200ms it's worth it.
[04:18] Aisha: in our SLA the 99th matters. so we want something that addresses tail.
[04:23] Ethan: agreed. question on workload shape — do agent spawns create new prefixes or reuse existing ones? any pattern?
[04:31] David: both. the system prompt is common across sessions, but agents create side-chats that often reuse initial semantic prefixes. sometimes we see a prefix id reused across sessions due to user templates.
[04:44] Marco: that reuse is good — reuse increases hit rate. problem arises when you have many unique prefixes of similar weight — no single hotset emerges and eviction churn increases.
[04:55] Lina: one diagnostic we suggest: compute prefix reuse entropy — basically the distribution skew. if top 10 prefixes account for >60% hits you can pin those with small memory cost. if it's uniform, you need to raise cache capacity or tune stride.
[05:12] Aisha: how do you compute prefix id in the runtime? we saw names like 'pref_0x23ab' in logs. is it deterministic per tokenization?
[05:20] Marco: deterministic based on normalized prompt hash + session namespace. collisions are rare but not impossible; we include a namespace to avoid cross-customer leakage.
[05:30] David: one weird thing — during a burst this morning eviction rate doubled for a subset of nodes while others stayed stable. any idea why?
[05:39] Lina: likely imbalanced hotset placement. our cluster scheduler tries to co-locate hot prefixes to nodes using affinity, but if your autoscaler rapidly spins up nodes without warm-up the new nodes start empty and see heavy page-ins, while older nodes serve from cache. did you see a scale event?
[05:54] David: yes, we had an autoscale event triggered by CPU.
[05:58] Ethan: autoscale + no warm-up is a common pattern. recommended fixes: pre-warm new nodes with pinned hot prefixes, or apply a gradual traffic ramp (probabilistic routing) so new nodes warm via sampled traffic rather than all at once.
[06:14] Rita: is that configuration available on Redwood Private? we plan to deploy in our VPC and need that control.
[06:20] Marco: yes, both Redwood Dedicated and Private have warm-up hooks. in Private you can run a sidecar prefetch agent to hydrate keys before routing heavy traffic. micro-configs differ but the primitive exists.
[06:33] Aisha: about on-disk paging — we saw our disk IO saturation on the NVMe was at 95% during page-ins. is there a recommended max page-in throughput?
[06:42] Lina: depends on NVMe perf, but rule of thumb is keep sustained page-in under 60% of peak IOPS to allow for bursts. if you see sustained high IO, you either need larger RAM, more aggressive prefetch, or faster storage (e.g., add an NVMe cache tier). also check compaction/deserialization costs — some models produce large serialized KV chunks that take CPU to unpack.
[07:03] David: can Redwood's runtime change serialization format? we saw some fragmentation in stored segments.
[07:10] Marco: we can choose a compact binary format with less metadata per segment — that reduces IO and memory overhead. it's a safe config change but needs testing for compatibility with quantized kernels. we'll include a recommended format in the playbook.
[07:25] Ethan: quick sanity check — anyone hitting request-level fallback to a smaller model during evictions? Rita?
[07:31] Rita: not yet, we've been trying to avoid model switching because it complicates correctness and session continuity.
[07:38] Lina: fallback is an option: when a page-in would exceed tail latency SLO, route to a smaller model or trimmed context variant. downside is semantic regression. we usually prefer prefetch + pinning first.
[07:52] Aisha: about prefix reuse, we have a multi-session orchestration where each user may open 5 sub-chats. can you recommend a heuristic for pin budget per session?
[08:03] Marco: a simple heuristic: reserve 10% of cache for session pins, allocate per session a small budget of top K prefixes (K proportional to active sub-chats). use LRU-with-weight that counts session pin weight higher so they only evict under extreme pressure.
[08:19] David: interesting, that may avoid the oscillation.
[08:22] Speaker 2: (transcription) there was a note about quantization kernel affinity—did we want to talk about that?
[08:28] Lina: yes briefly. q4 kernels have different memory/access patterns; some kernels prefer contiguous KV layouts. if your serialization fragments KV blocks across many small segments, kernel inefficiency can amplify latency. compacting to larger contiguous chunks helps.
[08:46] Aisha: we use Q4_HYBRID; we've seen a slight increase in token cost when switching to stride=1 but better tail. is that normal?
[08:54] Marco: yes — eager prefetch uses more memory but reduces mid-generation page-in penalties, which look like higher token CPU but lower wall-clock tail. cost calculus depends on your price metric — if you pay for latency-sensitive infra, eager may be cheaper from an SLA standpoint.
[09:12] Ethan: ok let's go over the agreed benchmark plan — Lina?
[09:16] Lina: we propose three runs on your staging: stride=1, stride=4, stride=8. each run uses identical traffic replay for 24h, capture: per-prefix hit ratio sample, eviction rate, page-in latency distribution, IO throughput, and node warm-up timeline. we'll provide the script. EmberTech to provide workload trace and staging credentials.
[09:36] Aisha: can we also capture session-level continuity errors? we had two incidents where context seemed to reset mid-session.
[09:44] Marco: yes add a continuity checker that hashes recent N tokens of state across reconstructions. if there's a mismatch during page-in, flag it.
[09:54] David: what about observability cost — if we add sampled per-prefix metrics will that blow up monitoring bills?
[10:01] Lina: sample at 1% to start. that usually gives signal without cardinality explosion. aggregate to histograms and roll up.
[10:09] Rita: any quick wins we can do right away?
[10:13] Marco: three quick wins: 1) pin top 5 prefixes (soft-pin), 2) reduce autoscale aggressiveness to allow warm-up, 3) enable a small eager-prefetch for system prompts. all low-risk.
[10:25] Aisha: can you share commands for pinning? we prefer infra-as-code.
[10:30] Ethan: yes we'll send a terraform snippet and runtime flags.
[10:33] Speaker 3: (background chatter)
[10:36] Lina: another important metric — prefix age distribution. if your top prefixes are very old they indicate stable hotset; if top prefixes churn rapidly you may be generating ephemeral prefixes per user which is harder to optimize.
[10:50] David: where would we see 'age' in logs? prefix creation timestamp?
[10:55] Marco: yes, include prefix.created_at and last_access_ts in events. we can compute age histogram in Grafana.
[11:03] Aisha: any security concerns with pinning? will pinned prefixes remain in RAM accessible to other customers on multi-tenant?
[11:09] Lina: on hosted multi-tenant we isolate namespaces; pinning only affects within-customer namespace. on Private you control the node so it's safe. but always use the namespace hash to ensure separation.
[11:21] Rita: you mentioned compact serialization earlier — will that break delta patches to the model? we sometimes do online updates.
[11:28] Marco: compact format is backward-compatible; delta patches are applied at higher layer and we keep an adapter to read old formats during a transition window. we'll point to the migration steps.
[11:39] Ethan: time check — we've got about 10 minutes, maybe run rapid-fire troubleshooting checklist?
[11:44] Lina: checklist: 1) collect workload traces (Aisha), 2) enable sampled prefix histograms (David), 3) run three stride experiments (shared script), 4) pre-warm policy for autoscale (ops policy), 5) set soft-pin defaults for system prompts.
[11:59] Aisha: that's actionable, thanks. one last dumb question — how much memory does one prefix roughly cost for 32k model?
[12:07] Marco: ballpark: each token KV entry depends on model head size but estimate 1.2–1.8 KB per token in q4 hybrid due to extra metadata. a prefix of 2k tokens ~ 2.4–3.6 MB. multiply by number of prefixes. that helps planning.
[12:26] David: good to have numbers.
[12:29] Rita: last ask — can Redwood run initial staging run for us and share results? we can give access but prefer Redwood to kick it off given tight ops.
[12:38] Ethan: we can run an initial smoke test with your creds and the script; we'll need an account and a short window. we'll also produce a short report.
[12:48] Aisha: thanks. we'll assemble the trace and creds by end of day Friday.
[12:52] Ethan: perfect. thanks everyone. we'll follow up with the scripts, terraform snippet, and Marco's playbook. meeting adjourned.
[12:58] Speaker 4: (transcript note) some words may be mis-typed by the transcript engine e.g., K-Vee vs KV.

source.title: EmberTech - embeddings + rerank API walkthrough
source.summary: AE + SE demo of Redwood hosted API focusing on embeddings + reranker flow for improving search relevance. Showed console quickstart, API examples, Optimize suggestions for batching and caching, and a live rerank example using EmberTech sample data. Discussed latency/cost tradeoffs, private VPC option, and security docs. Agreed next steps: share pricing, SOC2 docs, and run a 2-week pilot with 1M query sample. SE to run a benchmark with EmberTech dataset.
topics: Console demo: model selection and rollouts
action_items: Priya Natarajan: run a throughput/latency benchmark on EmberTech sample (due 2025-04-14)
Jordan Myers: send pricing spreadsheet and pilot T&Cs (due 2025-04-09)
Sofia Martinez: share 1M anonymized query logs and current rerank labels (due 2025-04-10)
next_steps: Share SOC2 type II and data handling docs
Provide console access for EmberTech sandbox project
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-04-07 15:00 UTC
Duration: 54 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Myers (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Sofia Martinez (EmberTech - Head of Search), Liam O'Connor (EmberTech - Staff ML Engineer), Ava Chen (EmberTech PM)

Summary (auto): Quick demo of Redwood hosted embeddings + reranker endpoints. Walkthrough of Console to create project, select model, run embeddings, and a re-rank pipeline. Discussed cost/latency and pilot next steps.

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Jordan from Redwood, AE for EmberTech, and with me is Priya from the SE team. How's everyone doing?
[00:06] Sofia: Good, thanks. Excited to see how this could help our search relevance problem.
[00:10] Priya: Awesome. Quick agenda: we'll do a quick console walkthrough, show the embeddings endpoint and then demo the rerank API using a small sample of your queries, and finish with Optimize recommendations for performance and cost.
[00:23] Liam: Sounds good. Our main constraint is 200ms p95 on search latency and keeping per-query cost sub-5 cents; we also need user PII to never leave our VPC.
[00:33] Jordan: Great callouts. We'll cover hosted options and also dedicated/vpc approaches. Note that for pilot we usually do hosted with private data retention settings, but we can detail the Private deployment for compliance later.
[00:45] Priya: I'm going to share my screen — console first. (screen noise) Here is a sandbox project. You can see the model catalog, we recommend embedding models tuned for retrieval first — in this demo we'll use rwd-embed-compact-v1.
[01:05] Speaker 1: Quick note, model names in the UI map to the underlying open checkpoints, and we surface perf profiles — p95, throughput, cost per embed.
[01:20] Sofia: Can you show how batching works? We do lots of small queries, but want to aggregate to reduce cost.
[01:28] Priya: Yep, under Optimize we have a batching slider; you can set target latency and it will suggest batch sizes and an autoscale policy. For example, target 200ms suggests micro-batch of 8 for this model and region.
[01:44] Liam: Micro-batch of 8 — does that change p95 significantly? We can't have spikes.
[01:50] Priya: There's a trade-off: batching reduces token cost and increases throughput, but it can add a bit to p95 for single-shot queries. We also have prefix caching and KV cash — sorry, KV cache — that reduces repeated prefix costs for multi-turn or contextual embeddings.
[02:06] Jordan: And we also have routing policies, so you can route low-priority bulk reranks to cheaper capacity and keep critical queries on low-latency hosts.
[02:16] Ava: Walk me through the rerank API payloads — what do we send and what do we get back?
[02:22] Priya: Sure. The flow we recommend: 1) call /embeddings to get vectors for documents and queries; 2) store doc vectors in your vector DB; 3) for a query, fetch top-k candidates from vector DB and then call /rerank with the query + candidate docs. The rerank endpoint returns a score and optional structured output if you ask for it.
[02:40] Priya: Example payload — query: "best noise cancelling headphones", candidates: [doc1, doc2, doc3], you can include doc metadata. We'll post the full sample payload after the call.
[02:52] Liam: Is rerank running on the same model or a special cross-encoder?
[02:57] Priya: It's a dedicated reranker model — rwd-rerank-v1 — optimized for short re-scoring. It's more compute per doc than embeddings but gives higher precision. In cases where you need lower cost, we have hybrid heuristics where we do a cheap embed similarity and then rerank top 10 only.
[03:12] Jordan: Let's do a live demo — Priya will paste a small sample from what you provided.
[03:17] (typing)
[03:28] [05:00] Priya: Ok, I'm issuing an embeddings call now. Here's curl: curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/embeddings -d '{"model":"rwd-embed-compact-v1","input":["how to reset password","return policy for orders","best wireless mouse"]}' — we get back vectors and IDs.
[05:21] Speaker 2: Transcript generator note: audio overlap.
[05:30] Priya: Now I'll index them into a sample vector index and then run rerank. Sending rerank payload... (keystroke sounds) Response: doc2 scored 0.92, doc1 0.58, doc3 0.12. So rerank clearly surfaces a more relevant doc for that query.
[06:00] Sofia: That looks promising. What's the end-to-end latency for that rerank call?
[06:06] Priya: For this small sample it's ~60ms for embeddings (per item with batching) and rerank call is 120ms — so end-to-end ~180ms in this test region. That should be under your 200ms p95 for typical loads, but we need to benchmark with your real distribution.
[06:25] Liam: Our distribution has a heavy tail — many long queries with embeddings of 512 tokens occasionally. How does token length affect cost and latency?
[06:33] Priya: Token length affects embedding cost linearly; long docs should be chunked and stored as multiple vectors. For latency, it increases inference time; Optimize will suggest quantization and lower-precision variants for longer docs if quality tolerates it.
[06:48] Jordan: Also worth noting the Dedicated pools if you need consistent p95 under 150ms — dedicated GPUs with pinned capacity.
[06:58] Ava: Security — can you confirm data residency and who can access logs?
[07:03] Jordan: We have audit logs in Console, role-based access controls (SSO/SAML), and optional Private deployment for full VPC isolation. For hosted, we support customer-managed KMS for data at rest encryption and per-tenant logs retention. We'll share SOC2 and data handling docs after the call.
[07:20] Sofia: For the pilot, we'd prefer that data not be used for model training; is that supported?
[07:26] Priya: Yes — we have an opt-out for product telemetry and training usage; your pilot project can be configured to disable data feed to upstream training.
[07:35] Jordan: Let's jump to Optimize tab — it runs an analysis of your workload and suggests batch sizes, cache TTLs, and quantization levels. For your sample, it suggested 16-batch for high throughput windows and enabling 4-bit quantization for price-sensitive rerank jobs.
[07:53] Liam: 4-bit — does that materially change rerank quality?
[07:57] Priya: In our tests, high-signal rerank tasks dropped ~1-2% in NDCG for 4-bit vs full precision, but token cost dropped by ~40-50%. We can A/B test during pilot to quantify for EmberTech.
[08:12] Speaker 1: Minor audio glitch.
[08:20] Priya: One more feature: automatic fallbacks — if a particular model is overloaded, requests can be routed to a cheaper or smaller variant with graceful degradation. You can also pin routes to regions for residency.
[08:34] Sofia: Ops question — how do we monitor per-query costs and attribute to product teams?
[08:39] Jordan: Console has token-level cost breakdown by route/model and you can tag requests with a 'team' header to see per-team usage.
[08:46] Ava: Can we get a demo of the console billing export?
[08:50] Jordan: Yes — we'll include sample billing CSVs in the follow-up.
[09:00] [12:00] Priya: Back to the rerank payload — here's a pitfall we've seen: customers often send full HTML content as candidates; that bloats token counts. We recommend pre-cleaning and storing lightweight metadata. Also chunk large docs.
[12:25] Liam: Good call — our crawler stores raw HTML. We'll need a pre-processing step.
[12:30] Priya: We'll share a snippet for cleaning and chunking.
[12:35] Sofia: Anything about vector DBs? We're using an internal FAISS cluster today.
[12:40] Priya: That's fine — you can continue to use FAISS. The rerank step is agnostic; we only require you to call our rerank endpoint with candidate docs. For scaling, consider paginating top-k and re-ranking top 100 for higher recall.
[12:55] Jordan: Ok let's cover pilot success metrics — what would make this pilot a success for EmberTech?
[13:02] Sofia: 1) 15% lift in click-through from top-3 results 2) p95 latency stays under 200ms 3) per-query cost under $0.05 for typical queries.
[13:14] Jordan: Good. We can instrument A/B tests for CTR and provide dashboards.
[13:20] Ava: Timeline — we'd prefer a kickoff next week and a two-week pilot. Can you support that?
[13:25] Jordan: Yes. Priya, can SE run the benchmark and we'll send pricing and pilot docs?
[13:30] Priya: Yep. I'll take the benchmark. I'll need the anonymized logs.
[13:34] Sofia: We'll share a 1M query sample, anonymized, by Friday.
[13:40] Jordan: Great. Any other technical concerns?
[13:44] Liam: One — cold start on rerank jobs when autoscaling. If you have sudden spikes, what's the mitigation?
[13:50] Priya: We support warm pools and keep-alive instances for critical routes; you can configure minimum replica counts on Dedicated or Reserved modes. For hosted, we can recommend a warm threshold and pre-warm hooks.
[13:59] Liam: Excellent.
[14:05] Jordan: Ok, recap of next steps: Jordan to send pricing and pilot T&Cs, Priya to run benchmark and share scripts, EmberTech to send logs and decide pilot cohort. We'll schedule the kickoff for next Tuesday slot.
[14:20] Ava: That works.
[14:25] Jordan: Thanks everyone, we'll follow up with links to SOC2, billing export sample, and the API payload examples.
[14:33] Sofia: Thanks — talk soon.

--- End of transcript ---

Notes: Transcription contains a few recognition errors (e.g., "KV cash" instead of "KV cache", speaker overlaps). The meeting lasted ~54 minutes per calendar. The demo included live API calls and console screen shares.

Discussed latency/cost tradeoffs, private VPC option, and security docs. Agreed next steps: share pricing, SOC2 docs, and run a 2-week pilot with 1M query sample. SE to run a benchmark with EmberTech dataset.
